nodes.name: Open
nodes.desc: Record is active and available for action — the entry point for most construction records. Indicates the item has been created and is part of the active workflow. Every record type has some version of 'open' as its initial actionable state.

nodes.name: In Progress
nodes.desc: Work has begun on the record but is not yet complete. Indicates active effort by a responsible party — distinguishes items being worked on from those sitting idle. Common on tasks, action plans, inspections, and punch items.

nodes.name: Draft
nodes.desc: Record exists but has not been formally issued into the workflow. Editable, not yet visible to reviewers or counterparties. The pre-submission holding state where the creator refines before committing.

nodes.name: Closed
nodes.desc: Record has completed its lifecycle — no further action required. Terminal state indicating the workflow has run its course, whether resolved successfully or not. Distinct from Void (cancelled) and Approved (decision).

nodes.name: Void
nodes.desc: Record has been invalidated or cancelled — the work was never completed, just abandoned. Distinct from Closed (completed lifecycle). Voided records should be excluded from most metrics but tracked as a signal of rework or process waste.

nodes.name: On Hold
nodes.desc: Record is paused, awaiting external input, owner decision, or other blocking condition. Not cancelled — expected to resume. Common when waiting for design clarification, material delivery, or client direction.

nodes.name: Overdue
nodes.desc: Record has passed its due date without reaching its expected next state. A computed status based on comparing due_date to current_date and record status. Indicates workflow stall requiring attention.

nodes.name: Pending
nodes.desc: Awaiting action from the next responsible party. The record has entered the workflow but the reviewer has not yet begun evaluation. Pre-action holding state that starts the response time clock.

nodes.name: Submitted
nodes.desc: Formally sent to the reviewer or approver — the official handoff point. Clock starts on contractual response time. The creator has completed their work and is waiting for a decision.

nodes.name: Under Review
nodes.desc: Actively being evaluated by the responsible party. Between submitted and decision. Some systems track this explicitly, others infer it from ball-in-court assignment.

nodes.name: Approved
nodes.desc: Accepted without conditions — authorizes the associated work, cost, or material to proceed. The positive terminal decision in an approval workflow. On financial records, triggers commitment of funds.

nodes.name: Approved as Noted
nodes.desc: Accepted with conditions, corrections, or minor modifications. Common on submittals — fabrication may proceed but noted changes must be incorporated. The 'yes, but' decision that avoids full rejection.

nodes.name: Rejected
nodes.desc: Not accepted — requires rework before resubmission. A hard stop that sends the record back to the originator. Resets the workflow cycle. Should always include rejection reason for tracking.

nodes.name: Revise and Resubmit
nodes.desc: Similar to rejected but with specific revision guidance — the reviewer has identified what needs to change. Softer than rejection, implies the approach is correct but execution needs refinement. Common on submittals.

nodes.name: Ready for Review
nodes.desc: Field work is complete and awaiting quality inspection, superintendent sign-off, or third-party verification. The handoff point between field crews and quality/management teams.

nodes.name: Conforming
nodes.desc: Inspection item meets the specified requirements — pass disposition. The field evaluator has verified the work complies with plans, specs, or standards. Moves the item toward closure.

nodes.name: Deficient
nodes.desc: Inspection item fails to meet requirements — requires corrective action. A formal finding that the installed work does not comply with plans, specifications, or applicable codes. Triggers rework cycle.

nodes.name: Not Applicable
nodes.desc: Record or inspection item does not apply in this context — excluded from evaluation. Used when an inspection checklist item is irrelevant to the specific work being inspected.

nodes.name: Workflow Step
nodes.desc: An individual stage in a multi-step approval or review process. Defines who must act, what action is required, and the sequence position. The atomic unit of workflow configuration.

nodes.name: Approval Chain
nodes.desc: Ordered sequence of reviewers and approvers that a record must pass through before reaching a terminal state. Defines the review hierarchy, required vs. optional reviewers, and fallback rules.

nodes.name: Ball in Court
nodes.desc: The party currently responsible for taking the next action on a record. Construction-specific accountability term — at any moment, someone 'has the ball'. Changes as the record moves through workflow steps.

nodes.name: Distribution List
nodes.desc: Set of parties notified when a record is created, changes status, or receives a response. Ensures relevant stakeholders stay informed without being in the approval chain. Notification, not action.

nodes.name: Workflow Response
nodes.desc: A reviewer's formal action at a workflow step — approve, reject, forward, return, or comment. The decision record that advances (or returns) the workflow. Timestamped for cycle time calculation.

nodes.name: Due Date
nodes.desc: The date by which action must be completed on a record or workflow step. Contractually significant — missed due dates can trigger liquidated damages, delay claims, or escalation.

nodes.name: Response Time
nodes.desc: The elapsed time between submission and response — either the contractual allowance or the actual measured duration. Critical for project pacing — slow responses cascade into schedule delays.

nodes.name: Escalation
nodes.desc: Elevation of a stalled workflow item to a higher authority when the responsible party fails to act within the allowed response time. The safety valve for stuck workflows.

nodes.name: Delegation
nodes.desc: Temporary or permanent transfer of approval authority from one party to another. Required when the primary reviewer is unavailable — prevents workflow stalls during vacations, role changes, or emergencies.

nodes.name: Sequential Approval
nodes.desc: Approvers review in a defined order — each must act before the next receives the item. Ensures layered review (PM → Architect → Owner). Most formal and slowest pattern.

nodes.name: Parallel Review
nodes.desc: Multiple reviewers evaluate simultaneously — all must respond before the workflow advances. Faster than sequential but requires all parties to be available. Common on design submittals with multiple discipline reviewers.

nodes.name: Submit and Approve
nodes.desc: Single submission followed by one or more approval decisions. The simplest and most common construction workflow pattern — the creator sends, the reviewer decides.

nodes.name: Review and Respond
nodes.desc: A question or information request requiring a formal written response. The RFI pattern — question asked, answer provided, response becomes part of the project record. Response may have cost/schedule impact.

nodes.name: Inspect and Disposition
nodes.desc: Field evaluation of installed work against defined criteria, resulting in a conforming/deficient judgment. The quality assurance pattern — inspector walks the work, checks each item, renders a disposition.

nodes.name: Create and Close
nodes.desc: Simplest lifecycle: an item is created, worked on, and closed. No formal approval chain — the responsible party resolves and closes. The punch list and field finding pattern.

nodes.name: Multi-Stage Financial Review
nodes.desc: A change progresses through independent budget, cost, and revenue stages — each with its own approval status. The change management pattern where a single event triggers parallel financial workflows across different ledgers.

nodes.name: Budget Stage
nodes.desc: The budget approval phase of a change event — tracks whether the budget impact has been formally recognized and approved. First stage in the three-stage financial workflow. Controls whether the project budget is adjusted.

nodes.name: Cost Stage
nodes.desc: The cost commitment phase of a change — tracks whether costs have been formally committed to subcontractors or vendors via change orders. Second stage. Controls whether the commitment is updated.

nodes.name: Revenue Stage
nodes.desc: The owner billing phase of a change — tracks whether the approved change has been incorporated into owner invoicing. Third and final stage. Controls whether the GC bills the owner for the change.

nodes.name: Change Event Lifecycle
nodes.desc: The end-to-end progression of a change from identification through budget recognition, cost commitment, and revenue billing. The composite view across all three financial stages for a single change event.

nodes.name: Mapped Status
nodes.desc: A standardized status field that normalizes domain-specific statuses into a common vocabulary for cross-record comparison. Allows consistent filtering and aggregation across record types that use different status taxonomies.

nodes.name: E-Signature Request
nodes.desc: A digital signing request sent through an integrated e-signature platform (e.g., DocuSign). Overlays the standard approval workflow — the record's internal status and the e-signature status track independently.

nodes.name: E-Signature Ball in Court
nodes.desc: The signer currently expected to act on a digital signature request. Mirrors the construction ball-in-court concept within the e-signature workflow — identifies who is holding up execution.

nodes.name: Average Approval Cycle Time
nodes.desc: Mean elapsed time from formal submission to final approval decision. The primary workflow efficiency metric. Formula: AVG(approval_date - submitted_date) in business days. Segment by record type, reviewer, and project phase.

nodes.name: Overdue Rate
nodes.desc: Percentage of records that exceed their due date before reaching the expected next state. Formula: COUNT(overdue_records) / COUNT(records_with_due_dates). Segment by record type and responsible party.

nodes.name: First-Pass Approval Rate
nodes.desc: Percentage of submissions approved on first review without requiring revision or resubmission. Formula: COUNT(approved_first_pass) / COUNT(total_submissions). Higher rate = better pre-submission quality.

nodes.name: Resubmission Rate
nodes.desc: Percentage of submissions that are rejected or returned for revision before eventual approval. Formula: COUNT(rejected_or_returned) / COUNT(total_submissions). The inverse complement of first-pass approval.

nodes.name: Ball-in-Court Aging
nodes.desc: Average time a record sits with the current responsible party before action is taken. Formula: AVG(current_date - bic_assignment_date) for active records. Identifies the slowest link in the workflow chain.

nodes.name: Status Dwell Time
nodes.desc: Average time a record spends in each status before transitioning to the next state. Identifies which statuses are bottlenecks in the workflow. Formula: AVG(next_status_date - current_status_date) per status.

nodes.name: Workflow Completion Rate
nodes.desc: Percentage of records that reach a terminal state (Closed, Approved, Complete) vs. remaining active or stalled. Formula: COUNT(terminal_status) / COUNT(total_created) over a time period.

nodes.name: Escalation Rate
nodes.desc: Percentage of workflow items that require elevation to a higher authority because the assigned party failed to act within the allowed time. Formula: COUNT(escalated) / COUNT(total_actionable). Higher rate = more workflow friction.

nodes.name: Process Bottleneck Detection
nodes.desc: Identifies workflow bottlenecks by analyzing approval cycle times across submittals, RFIs, and observations. Measures time-in-status for each workflow stage: submittal submission-to-approval, RFI creation-to-response, observation assignment-to-closure. Ball-in-court analysis reveals which parties (architect, owner, PM, subcontractor) consistently slow the workflow. Status dwell time by record type and responsible party identifies systemic delays vs. one-off slowdowns. Overdue rates by workflow type and reviewer enable targeted process improvement. Cross-referenced with project phase to distinguish early-project bottlenecks (design coordination) from late-project bottlenecks (closeout backlog).
